title: Progressive dyspnea and leg swelling

chart.oneLiner: 68M with HTN, prior MI, and T2DM presents with one week of progressive dyspnea on exertion and bilateral leg swelling.

opening: I just can't catch my breath like I used to, doc — and my legs have blown up.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 68-year-old man, tired and mildly anxious, breathing a little hard. Short, natural sentences. Cooperative, reliable historian. Retired mechanic, lives alone.

HPI:
- Dyspnea on exertion worsening over ~1 week, much worse the last 3 days. A month ago fine; now winded crossing a room.
- Orthopnea (3 pillows / recliner) and PND (wakes ~1hr after lying down gasping, relieved by sitting up).
- Bilateral leg swelling to the shins, worse by evening.
- Mild dry cough, no sputum, no wheeze, no hemoptysis.
- ~6 lb weight gain this week; clothes tight; mild early satiety; less urine output.
- NO chest pain, NO pleuritic pain, NO fever/chills, NO calf pain, NO recent travel/immobilization, NO palpitations, NO syncope.

OLDCARTS: gradual onset over a week; 'air hunger' not pain/tightness; constant, worse with exertion & lying flat; relieved by rest & sitting up; worse at night.

PMH: hypertension, type 2 diabetes, prior inferior MI ~4 yrs ago (stent placed), hyperlipidemia.
MEDICATIONS: lisinopril 20mg, metoprolol succinate 50mg, atorvastatin 40mg, metformin 1000mg BID, aspirin 81mg, AND furosemide 40mg daily — BUT ran out ~10 days ago and never refilled.
ADHERENCE/WHAT CHANGED: ran out of the water pill (furosemide) ~10 days ago; ate salty holiday meal + lots of takeout last week.
ALLERGIES: none. FAMILY: father MI at 60, mother HTN.
SOCIAL: former smoker (quit 4 yrs, ~20 pack-yr), 1–2 beers weekends, no drugs, recent high-salt diet, independent.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY the system/maneuver requested; give normals when asked):
- General: older man, mild resp effort at rest, alert.
- Vitals: BP 158/92, HR 98 reg, RR 22, 37.0°C, SpO2 90% RA (95% on 2L).
- HEENT: mucous membranes moist, no scleral icterus.
- Neck: JVP elevated ~10 cm, no carotid bruits.
- Cardiac: regular, normal S1/S2, audible S3 gallop, no murmur, PMI mildly displaced.
- Lungs: bibasilar crackles to mid-lung fields bilaterally, no wheeze.
- Abdomen: soft, mild RUQ fullness, no tenderness, no fluid wave.
- Extremities: 2+ pitting edema to knees bilaterally, warm, no calf tenderness/asymmetry.
- Skin: no rash/cyanosis. Neuro: A&Ox3, no focal deficits.
- Special: hepatojugular reflux POSITIVE; orthostatics no change; calf tenderness/Homans negative.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state, suggest, or hint at a diagnosis.
- Answer ONLY what is asked; focused realistic answers, not a data dump.
- Do NOT volunteer the missed furosemide or dietary salt unless asked about medications, refills, adherence, what changed, or diet.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.BMP: Na 136, K 4.2, Cl 101, HCO3 26, BUN 28, Cr 1.4 (baseline 1.1), Glu 162 — mild Cr bump.
labs.Troponin: 0.04 ng/mL, mildly elevated, STABLE on repeat — demand, not rising.

cxr.findings: Cardiomegaly, cephalization, Kerley B lines, interstitial edema, small bilateral pleural effusions.

searchTerms: chest radiograph Cardiomegaly cephalization Kerley lines

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential diagnosis (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: ADHF (leading,7; accepts CHF exacerbation/HFrEF/volume overload). ACS (CANT-MISS,5). PE (CANT-MISS,5). Pneumonia (core,2). COPD exacerbation (core,2). Anemia (bonus,1). Named leading dx w/ justification (core,3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Acute decompensated heart failure (HFrEF). Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: ACS; PE. Also reasonable: Pneumonia; COPD exacerbation.

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: EKG (CANT-MISS,3). Troponin (CANT-MISS,2). CXR (core,3). BNP (core,3). BMP (core,2). CBC (core,2). PENALTY: shotgun panel w/ no reasoning (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; Troponin; CXR; BNP; BMP; CBC.

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings systematically.
steps.rubric: EKG findings = sinus rhythm ~96, OLD inferior infarct Q waves in II/III/aVF, NO acute ST elevation/ischemia. CREDIT: states rate & rhythm (4); identifies old inferior Q waves / prior infarct (5); correctly notes NO acute ST-elevation/ischemia (4); systematic coherent read (2). PENALTY: over-calling an acute STEMI/acute ischemia (-3).
keyFindings: identifies old inferior Q waves / prior infarct
criticalFindings: Sinus rhythm ~96. Old inferior infarct: Q waves in II/III/aVF. NO acute ST elevation or ischemia.
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus rhythm ~96. Old inferior infarct: Q waves in II/III/aVF. NO acute ST elevation or ischemia.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Sinus rhythm ~96. Old inferior infarct: Q waves in II/III/aVF. NO acute ST elevation or ischemia.

coreActions.item: identifies old inferior Q waves / prior infarct

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: CXR findings = cardiomegaly, pulmonary venous congestion/cephalization, interstitial edema, Kerley B lines, small bilateral pleural effusions. CREDIT: cardiomegaly (2); pulmonary congestion/cephalization/interstitial edema (4); Kerley B lines (2); bilateral pleural effusions (2).
criticalFindings: Cardiomegaly, cephalization, Kerley B lines, interstitial edema, small bilateral pleural effusions.
commonMistakes: Failing to comment on rate/rhythm or exposure
steps.idealAnswer: Cardiomegaly, cephalization, Kerley B lines, interstitial edema, small bilateral pleural effusions.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Cardiomegaly, cephalization, Kerley B lines, interstitial edema, small bilateral pleural effusions.

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Pulling together the labs and your EKG/CXR reads, what is your leading diagnosis and reasoning?
steps.rubric: Commits to ADHF as leading (5). Explains mild troponin as demand not primary ACS (3). Identifies precipitant — missed diuretic/dietary salt (bonus,2).

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: IV loop diuretic (CANT-MISS,6; accepts IV furosemide/Lasix/diuresis). Daily weights & strict I/Os (3). Supplemental O2 prn (2). Na & fluid restriction (3). Order echo (3). GDMT optimization (4; ARNI/ACE/ARB/beta blocker/MRA/SGLT2). Address precipitant + adherence counseling (2). Telemetry/cards follow-up (bonus,2). PENALTY: IV fluids/bolus to a volume-overloaded patient (-4).

penalties.item: IV fluids/bolus to a volume-overloaded patient

unsafeActions: IV fluids/bolus to a volume-overloaded patient

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 68M with HTN, prior MI, and T2DM presents with one week of progressive dyspnea on exertion and bilateral leg swelling. The diagnosis is Acute decompensated heart failure (HFrEF), reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Abdomen: soft, mild RUQ fullness, no tenderness, no fluid wave.

reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Acute decompensated heart failure (HFrEF) while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Acute decompensated heart failure (HFrEF) and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.

Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.
